segments.s720-g17: چو دانسته شد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' + دانسته شد = 'was known' (passive/auxiliary sense). آتشی بر فروخت places the noun 'آتشی' (a fire) before the verb فروخت (here: 'was kindled/gleamed'), with بر = 'upon/on' marking the direction/location of the burning.
segments.s720-g18: سرای here refers to the household/house (the provided lemma senses don't include that meaning, so sense is marked below accordingly). و = 'and'. همه = 'all/everyone'. بر = 'upon/on'. زن او = 'his woman/wife(s)' (او marked possessively). بسوخت is perfective/passive-style past 'was burned' (the verb expresses the burning as a completed event).
segments.s720-g19: دو فرزند او را is the definite/direct-object phrase 'his two sons' (او = his; را marks the object). بر آتش نهاد uses نهاد (past of نهادن) = 'placed/laid' with بر آتش = 'upon the fire' (word order: object before the verb, typical in Persian narrative).
segments.s720-g20: همه چیز او را = 'all his things' (هرچه داشت); را is the object marker. به تاراج داد = 'gave to pillage' i.e. 'allowed/gave everything to be looted' (داد = 'gave', تاراج = 'pillage' noun indicating the fate of the goods).
segments.s720-g21: ازان پس = 'after that/thereupon'. چو ... رسید = temporal clause 'when ... came'; نوبت به خاتون رسید is an idiom meaning 'the turn came to the lady' (رسید = 'reached/arrived' in the sense of 'it was her turn').
segments.s720-g22: ز پرده = 'from the curtain/veil' (poetic z = from). به گیسوش has the clitic -ش on گیسو = 'her hair' (گیسوش = 'her hair'); بیرون کشید is a compound verbal idea 'pulled/drew out' (بیرون = out; کشید = drew/pulled). Note: کشید is past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw'.
segments.s720-g23: به ایوان کشید = 'she brought/drew out onto the porch/iwan' (به + place + verb 'کشید' = moved/dragged/placed there). آن همه گنج اوی = 'all that treasure of his' (اوی is an archaic possessive form 'his'). Word order places the location first (به ایوان) then the object and verb.
segments.s720-g24: نکرد ایچ = 'did not at all (do anything)' where نکرد is the negated past of کردن. یاد = 'mention/remembrance' and از introduces the source/subject of that mention; در رنج اوی = 'in/of his suffering' (اوی = 'his'). The whole phrase means 'she made no mention whatsoever of his suffering.'
segments.s720-g9: بگفت is a past narrative verb meaning “he said” (archaic/poetic past form). آنک is a subordinating demonstrative (‘that/when/which’) introducing the reported clause. بهرام یل را is an agent/name (Bahram) plus the noun یل (‘hero’) with the object/topic marker را, and رسید is the simple past ‘reached/arrived’ that completes the subordinate clause.
segments.s720-g10: بشد is the past copular/verb meaning ‘it became’ (here translated ‘they became’ as a narrative result). زار و گریان are predicative adjectives ‘wretched and weeping’ that follow the verb. هران functions as a relative/indefinite phrase (‘هر آن’ = ‘whoever/each of them’). کوشنید here functions as the relative clause verb ‘who heard’ (poetic fused form); none of the provided sense entries matches this exact usage, so it is treated as a relative ‘who heard it’.
segments.s720-g11: همه is the plural subject ‘all/everyone’. چین برو is a poetic adverbial phrase meaning roughly ‘thus/in that manner’ (poetic inversion); the separate listed senses do not supply this sense. زار و گریان شدند is the predicate: two adjectives ‘wretched and weeping’ followed by شدند, the past plural of شدن ‘to become.’
segments.s720-g12: This is a nominal phrase turned predicative: ابی (blue) and تیز (sharp/fierce) are adjectives modifying آتش (fire), and بریان (‘burning, roasted’) is another adjectival/participial modifier; شدند is the plural past of شدن ‘they became’ — so ‘they became a blue, fierce, burning fire.’ Word order here is poetic (adjectives and modifiers clustered before the copula).
segments.s720-g13: یکایک is an emphatic distributive adverb ‘one by one / each and every’. همه کار او را places the direct object ‘all his affairs’ (کار = affairs/deeds, او = his, را = definite object marker). بساخت is a past transitive verb ‘he arranged/put in order’ (poetic past); the supplied sense list does not give the verbal sense, so the lexical entry provided is inadequate for this context.
segments.s720-g14: نگه کرد is a compound verbal phrase: نگه (here ‘keeping/watch’) + کرد (past of کردن ‘to do’) = ‘kept watch / observed’. کاین introduces the following noun phrase (‘that/this’); بدبریشان is an adjectival/nominal phrase ‘that ill‑fated one/their ill‑fortune’ (بدبر = ill‑fated, -شان = their). که تاخت is a relative clause ‘who rushed/charged’ (تاخت = past ‘galloped/rushed/attacked’), explaining why he kept watch.
segments.s720-g15: قلون is a proper name here (not the common noun sense), so it functions as the subject. را is the object/topic marker attached to the name in this poetic construction. به توران is a locative phrase ‘in/at Turan’. دو فرزند بود literally ‘there were two children’ or ‘he had two sons’ — بود is the past copula indicating existence/possession in past tense.
segments.s720-g16: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional/partitive phrase ‘from every kind/lineage’. خویش is ‘his kin/relations’ (خوی + possessive ش = his), و links items (‘and’), پیوند is ‘bond/tie/ally’, and بود is the past copula ‘there was’ — together: ‘from every kind there were kin and bonds’ (i.e. he had relatives and ties in every lineage).
segments.s720-g25: فرستاد is a third-person singular past-tense verb (“he sent”). هرسو is an adverbial phrase placed after the verb that means “everywhere/in every direction.” هیونان is the plural of هیون and here functions as the direct object (postverbal object order is common in poetry), meaning ferocious creatures/monsters. مست is an adjective following the noun it modifies, ‘drunk’ or ‘intoxicated’ (used here adjectivally to qualify the creatures).
segments.s720-g26: نیامدش is composed of the past negative نیامد (“did not come”) plus the enclitic pronoun -ش meaning “to him/it”; the construction therefore means “did not come to him/it.” خراد is a noun placed after the verb for emphasis and here means “wisdom/reason.” برزین is a proper name in the oblique position (the beneficiary/possessor), and بدست is used in the sense “into the hand / into the possession of.” Together the clause means literally “wisdom did not come to him into Borzīn’s hand.”
segments.s720-g27: همه هرچ is a doubled expression of totality — “all/everything” (همه) plus هرچ/هرچه (“whatever/whatsoever”) — emphasizing inclusiveness. در چین is a locative prepositional phrase “in China.” را here is the Persian definite/object marker attached for emphasis to what follows, and بنده بود is the past copula + noun “was (a) servant/servants.” The overall word order is typical poetic inversion: subject/quantifier + locative phrase + object marker + predicate.
segments.s720-g28: به is a preposition here marking direction/beneficiary (“to/for”); پوشیدشان is a past-tense verb پوشید (“he clothed/covered”) with the enclitic pronoun -شان as its direct object “them.” جامه‌های is the plural of جامه “garments” and کبود is the adjectival color “dark-blue/azure,” placed after the noun in the usual Persian order. Together: “he clothed them in dark-blue garments.”
segments.s720-g29: بیک چند is a fixed poetic expression meaning something like “for a short while / for some time” (here treated as a temporal phrase); syntactically it functions as an adverbial phrase. با is the preposition “with,” سوک is a noun meaning “mourning/dirge,” and بهرام is the proper name of the person being mourned. بود is the past copula “was.” The clause thus says that there was (some) mourning for Bahram for a time.
segments.s720-g30: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause here (“which / for which / that”), followed by خاقان (khaghan, the ruler) as the subject. ازان is a prepositional/pronominal demonstrative meaning “from that / because of that / of that affair,” کار is the noun “deed/affair,” and بدنام بود is the compound predicate “was notorious / became infamous.” Together the clause explains that the khaghan became notorious because of that affair.
segments.s720-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). بشنید is a past narrative verb 'he heard' (verb before subject, a common poetic inversion). که begins the reported-content clause 'that ...' and بهرام را is the topical/accusative-marked phrase ('Bahram' marked by را as the person affected by whatever happened).
segments.s720-g2: چه آمد is a fixed classical formula meaning 'what befell / what happened'. بروی is the verb 'to go' (here reporting that he went/left), and از پی together means 'in pursuit of' — پی takes the sense 'pursuit'. نام را marks the object/topic 'fame' (را marks it as definite/emphatic). The whole phrase is the content of the 'که' clause.
segments.s720-g3: چوآن is the temporal connector 'when/as soon as'. نامه is the grammatical subject 'the letter'. نزدیک خاقان here marks destination/presence 'to/near the khagan', and رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached' (the letter reached the khagan).
segments.s720-g4: شد functions as 'became' (resulting state). از indicates cause ('from/because of') and درد 'pain' is emotional sorrow here; گریان is a predicative adjective/participle 'weeping'. هران کان شنید is a compressed archaic/poetic expression meaning 'all those who heard it' (هران = هر آن, کان here is an archaic connector, شنید 'heard').
segments.s720-g5: از آن آگهی is an ablative phrase 'from that news'. شد is 'became' and دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش). پر ز درد is an idiomatic adjective phrase 'full of pain/sorrow' where پر = 'full (of)' and ز (poetic variant of از) marks the source 'of/with'.
segments.s720-g6: دو دیده 'two eyes' (literal); پر از خون is again 'full of blood' (پر + از + خون). و links the two descriptions and رخ لاژورد is a verbless/elliptical nominal phrase 'a face (like) lapis-lazuli' — classical Persian often omits the copula, so the face is described as lapis-colored.
segments.s720-g7: ازان کار او is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of that deed of his' (ازان = از آن). در شگفتی بماند means 'remained in astonishment' — بماند is used here in the narrative sense 'he remained/was left', with در شگفتی 'in astonishment' as the locative/ state.
segments.s720-g8: جهاندیدگان را همه is the object phrase 'all the worldly-wise / experienced men' (را marks them as the object/topic). پیش خواند is a compound sense 'called/summoned before (him)' — پیش 'in the presence of' modifies خواند (past 'called/summoned').

sentences.s720-s9: When it became known that a fire had been kindled, the house and all his women were burned.
sentences.s720-s10: They placed his two sons upon the fire; they gave all his possessions to be plundered.
sentences.s720-s11: After that, when the turn came to the lady, she drew her hair out from behind the curtain.
sentences.s720-s12: She brought all his treasure out to the iwan; she did not at all make mention of his suffering.
sentences.s720-s5: He said that when Bahram reached the hero, those who heard it became wretched and wept.
sentences.s720-s6: All of them thus became wretched and weeping; they became like a blue, sharp, blazing fire.
sentences.s720-s7: One by one he put all his affairs in order and kept watch, for that ill‑fated man who had rushed (upon them).
sentences.s720-s8: Qalun had two sons in Turan; from every kind there were kin and ties.
sentences.s720-s13: He sent drunken creatures in every direction; wisdom did not come into Borzīn’s hand.
sentences.s720-s14: All that was in China and its domain were servants; he clothed them in dark-blue garments.
sentences.s720-s15: For a short time there was mourning for Bahram; that affair made the khagan notorious.
sentences.s720-s1: When the khagan heard what had happened to Bahram — that he had gone off in pursuit of fame.
sentences.s720-s2: As soon as that letter reached the khagan, he became from pain weeping, (as) all who heard (it).
sentences.s720-s3: From that news his heart became full of sorrow; his two eyes were full of blood and his face (looked) lapis-colored.
sentences.s720-s4: Because of that deed of his he remained in astonishment; he summoned all the worldly-wise men to his presence.
